title: 2024/11/14 - Petitions
displayTitle: 2024-11-14 - Petitions Committee - Petition of Holly Brooker: Mandate Internet Service Providers to block rape, bestiality and slavery porn
topic: Petition of Holly Brooker: Mandate Internet Service Providers to block rape, bestiality and slavery porn
description: 14 November 2024 | Petitions Committee Petition of Holly Brooker: Mandate Internet Service Providers to block rape, bestiality and slavery porn Submitters Holly Brooker Department of Internal Affairs - Nick Law, Policy Manager, Media Content & Online Safety - Jared Mullen, Director, Digital Safety - John Michael, Deputy Director Operations, Digital Safety Classification Office - Caroline Flora, Chief Censor - Rupert Ablett-Hampson, Deputy Chief Censor Internet Service Providers Association of New Zealand - David Haynes, Chief Executive
descriptionSummary: Petition of Holly Brooker: Mandate Internet Service Providers to block rape, bestiality and slavery porn. Holly Brooker. Department of Internal Affairs

Alright. As, uh, Mr. Law. Yes. Yes. Taylor co committee chair members. Um, just to introduce myself properly, I'm Nick Law Policy Manager internal affairs for a team called Media Content and Online Safety. And with me is Jared Mullen. Jared is the director of the department's Digital Safety Regulatory Group.

Thank you for the opportunity to speak to you today about the petition to require internet service providers to filter rate content, child sex, abuse, and material and bestiality content. We'd like to acknowledge the petitioners Holly Brooker and Joe uh, Robertson for the effort they have put into raising awareness of the risk of harm to children by exposure to illegal material and the effort in gathering almost 11,000 signatures on this petition.

I don't intend to read through the department's written submission, but we will highlight some key areas for the committee to consider, such as what will be required to expand and mandate the filter as proposed in the petition. Although I do appreciate the submitters have just, uh, altered that slightly, but I will stick to as the petition as it was written. I'll first go through a bit of background on the filter that the department operates.

The department administers the classification act and this sets out what material is considered objectionable or illegal, such as child sexual abuse material, which we often refer to as CS A. We also operate the digital child exploitation filtering system to prevent and deter access to child sexual abuse material.

And we call this D SEFs. The D SEFs blocks access to webpages identified as hosting CS a and as one of several tools that we use to take, uh, to address this type of content. It is an early intervention to stop people from inadvertent low motivation access to CS a and enables an education response for people curious about this content, the filter will not stop the most determined people from accessing this content.

And for those people, we must rely on different approaches. Many internet service providers are voluntarily using DCFS on their services, and this covers about 95% of internet users in New Zealand. The voluntary takeup of the filter works because child sexual abuse material can be confidently identified and is universally considered to be abhor.

The filter will soon undergo a significant upgrade through an agreement with the Internet Watch Foundation, which we expect to be operational in the first quarter of next year. This upgrade will increase the size of the department's filter list from a few hundred URLs hosting CSA to tens of thousands. It will also reduce the time and resource required to manually update the list, which means our investigators can focus on the often lengthy and highly sensitive investigations into digital child exploitation.

Our assessment of the petition's request is that the changes would be a significant departure from the current voluntary arrangement. The scope of the change requested in the petition would require legislative amendments to the Classification Act and likely consequential amendments to other acts. The classification Act was enacted in 1993 well before the internet as we know it today.

The ACT does not include provisions for a mandated internet filtering system, nor does it empower the government or direct, uh, government to direct internet service providers to filter and block content on their services. To do both of these things as requested in the petition, would require a clear mandate from Parliament under a mandated system.

Compliance and transparency would also need to be prescribed in legislation to empower the department or other regulatory body to monitor the ISPs implementation of the filter. We are also concerned that extending the filter to more categories could unintentionally acc uh, restrict access to legal material rape am bestiality content sometimes will not meet the threshold for objectionable, unlike child sexual abuse material, which is always obviously illegal.

For example, some adults consensually create content that depicts rape. Unfortunately, there are also incident where people will record real rape and share it as content differentiating between the two. And the effect of this content is complex. We could not confidently block rape or bestiality content in the same way that we do for child sexual abuse material.

If we were to do this, we would run the risk of blocking legal content and violating the Bill of Rights Act, which includes the right to freedom of expression and access to information. So while rape and bestiality content are categories of objectionable material under section three of the Classification Act, distinguishing the two is a matter of careful consideration by experts like the department's investigators and the classification office and the this, their expertise is recognized in section four of the act.

If rape and bestiality content were included in a filter, then we would need to consider what would be an acceptable risk of over blocking material and how would that be mitigated to protect the government and internet service providers from liability if legal content were blocked? The petition is noted in their submission that existing machine learning technology can scan and detect sites hosting rape and BCL content.

We are not aware of technology that can do this to the level of accuracy needed for a government filter without human review. First, automated detection and filtering tools work better for private use, for example, in households where a higher error rate is accepted by customers because they are the ones exercising control over their internet use.

The other complication with automated filtering is that developing filtering lists requires content to train the machine. Developing this technology in New Zealand privately would be difficult 'cause it would need to be trained on very large sets of images and videos. The Classification Act does not authorize proactive access to objectional material, including for the use of training and auditing a machine learning tool.

And the department does not have the authority to grant legal access in this way. The classification office can grant exemptions for specific illegal content, but it cannot grant an exemption to generally access a class of the legal content. Finally, expanding a mandating filter would also require significant departmental resources to operate it, particularly because automated content detection has its limits and should not replace human oversight.

This level of activity cannot be funded from baseline funding for which the department uses for the highest priority work like investigations into child sexual abuse material. That concludes our comments. We do share a common goal with the petitioners to reduce harm from illegal content and we commend their work in this area. Thank you again for inviting the department to submit and present today.

But I will start with a bit of background and clarity about our role. So we are an independent crown entity and content regulator. Our roles and functions are set out in the classification Act 1993.

And broadly we classify material that might need to be restricted or banned. This can include films, books, videos, games. And importantly in this context, online content, uh, potentially objectionable or illegal content is it comes to us via crown agencies in the court. So that includes DIA police and sometimes customs.

So our classification function is quasi-Judicial and classifications are legally enforceable so that a determination that a publication is objectionable might form a a, a large part of a criminal charge.

So we are not responsible for investigations or enforcement of the classification act. That's the role of DIA in police. So this petition raises very valid concerns about access to harmful or objectionable material online. Our research shows that New Zealanders find it difficult to protect children and people online.

So in our view and in the spirit of the classification Act, content that is illegal under New Zealand law should not be freely available.

Our view that is that any mandatory filter would require a greater level of certainty that content being blocked was in fact objectionable under New Zealand law to minimize the risk of, of legal content being blocked.
